pmos

Retention — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Growth. Retention is the part of the growth engine that everything else compounds on top of — the practitioner canon is unusually unanimous that it is the core, and unusually divided on how to measure and move it. Alex Schultz and Brian Balfour both make retention the load-bearing wall: a flattening retention curve is the operational definition of product/market fit, and retention is an output you improve by fixing inputs, not by brainstorming directly. The diagnostic toolkit splits into the retention curve and the cohort triangle (where the signal lives across cohorts, not in the average), the power user curve (the distribution the average hides), and the growth-accounting lifecycle (new / current / resurrected / dormant). A recurring trap is measuring the wrong kind of retention for your product's natural frequency, or trusting a blended DAU/MAU ratio. On the causal side, three camps explain why users stick — activation/aha (Sean Ellis), habit formation (Nir Eyal), and accruing benefits / mounting losses (Sarah Tavel) — and the tactical literature (Lenny Rachitsky, Amplitude) keeps landing on the same answer: the biggest, most durable wins come from the early experience, and resurrection only pays off once the curve has stopped trending to zero. Where the field genuinely disagrees — retention-as-PMF nuance, correlation vs causation in aha hunting, benchmark comparability — this primer flags it. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 16 verified sources.

Why retention is the core of growth

The strongest framing of retention's primacy comes from Alex Schultz, who ran growth at Facebook: retention is "the single most important thing for growth," and the reason is mechanical rather than rhetorical. Every acquisition channel pours users into the top of the funnel, but only retention decides how many are still there a month later. A leaky bucket doesn't fill no matter how fast you pour — so the growth-hacking and virality tactics teams reach for first only matter once the bucket holds water. Schultz's blunt corollary is that most teams misdiagnose where they are: "they don't actually have product/market fit when they think they do," and pouring acquisition spend onto a product that doesn't retain just burns money faster.

Brian Balfour, who built Reforge around the growth-loop model, sharpens this into a structural claim: retention sits at the center of the engine because it feeds every other loop. Retained users are the ones who refer, who expand revenue, and who generate the content and network effects that pull the next cohort in. His most useful reframe is that "Retention Is An Output" — not a thing you improve by holding a retention brainstorm, but the downstream result of getting the inputs right, activation chief among them. This is why so much of the serious retention literature is actually about activation, onboarding, and habit: those are the levers; retention is the readout.

The practical consequence for a senior PM is a sequencing discipline. Before optimizing any acquisition channel or referral loop, you establish whether the curve flattens — because, in Schultz's words, without that flattening "none of the growth hacking or virality tactics that people love to talk about will work." Retention is both the first thing to measure and the last thing most teams fix, and that inversion is where a lot of growth budget dies.

The retention curve and product/market fit

The retention curve is the canonical artifact of this whole field: plot the percentage of a cohort still active against days (or weeks) since signup, and the shape tells you whether you have a business. Userpilot reads it in three movements — a steep early drop as users who never reach value fall away (their example halves "from 26.5% on Day 1 to 12% by Day 7"), then either a flattening plateau or a continued slide to zero.

The plateau is the whole game. A curve that flattens means a stable core of users have folded the product into their routine — "the flat section shows your product stickiness." Schultz states the test in its purest form: a retention curve that "asymptotes to a line parallel to the x-axis" is the operational signature of product/market fit, because it proves that some segment finds durable, repeated value. The height of that asymptote is the size of your real business; everything above it on day one is tourists.

Two cautions practitioners attach to this. First, the curve is an output: it diagnoses fit but doesn't tell you which lever moved it, which is why the cohort and lifecycle views below exist. Second, "flattening" is a judgment call on real, noisy data — early cohorts are small and the plateau can take weeks to declare itself. Balfour's nuance matters here too: the curve's acceptable shape depends on the business model, so a flattening curve is necessary evidence of fit but not a universal pass mark — what's good for a daily social app is failure for enterprise SaaS.

Reading cohort retention

A single blended retention number averages away the thing you most need to see: whether the product is getting better or worse over time. The cohort triangle fixes this. As Olga Berezovsky lays it out, you build a table showing "how many users registered each week and how many of them were retained in each subsequent week," conditionally color-coded because without the formatting "cohorts are difficult to read." Each row is a signup cohort; each column is a lifecycle age.

The discipline is to read the triangle two ways. Reading down a column compares different cohorts at the same age — week-4 retention for the January cohort versus the March cohort — which is how you detect whether product and onboarding changes are actually lifting retention for newer users. Reading across a row traces one cohort's decay over its own lifetime. Berezovsky's key point is that the insight lives in the differences across cohorts, not in any single average, and that retention is fundamentally an output metric reflecting "the cumulative product experience" — the flattening point reveals the natural retention equilibrium and, with it, your real pool of opportunity.

From there, behavioral cohorts turn diagnosis into a lever hunt. Amplitude shows the move with a music app: "almost 40% of all users churn within one day," but "only about 15% of users who favorite three or more songs drop off after day one." Inverting the cohort (users who never favorited) and combining behaviors (favoriting and joining a community lifts retention above 75%, versus under 25% with neither) isolates which early actions separate the retained from the churned. The essential caveat — the one that separates rigorous teams from cargo-cult ones — is that these are correlations until an A/B test proves that causing the behavior raises retention. The cohort tells you where to look; it does not certify the lever.

Choosing the right retention metric

One of the most consequential and least discussed decisions is which definition of "retained" you adopt, because the wrong one can make a healthy product look dying or a dying one look fine. Amplitude distinguishes three:

  • N-day retention counts a user as retained only if they return on a specific day after day 0 — Day 1, Day 7, Day 30. It's strict and well-suited to products you expect people to use on a regular cadence, "like daily for a mobile game, or weekly for an exercise app."
  • Unbounded (rolling) retention counts a user as retained if they returned on a given day or any time after. It forgives gaps, which makes it the honest choice for products with naturally sporadic use — grocery delivery, travel, tax software — where N-day would brand a perfectly happy user as churned for skipping a Tuesday.
  • Bracketed retention buckets time into custom intervals (multi-day, weekly, monthly) and counts a user active if they showed up anywhere in the bracket — a middle path you tune to the product's rhythm.

The load-bearing principle is the matching one: pick the retention type that matches your product's natural usage frequency. A team that ships a sporadic-use product but reports N-day retention will watch a scary-looking curve and over-invest in re-engagement the product never needed; a daily-habit product measured on unbounded retention will flatter itself and miss real decay. This is a definitional choice, not a dashboard setting — and getting it wrong quietly corrupts every downstream decision the metric feeds.

The power user curve and the smile

Retention curves and DAU/MAU both collapse the engagement distribution into a line or a ratio, and that collapse hides the most important fact about many products: how concentrated engagement is. Andrew Chen's power user curve — the "L30" activity histogram — restores it by plotting users by the total number of days they were active in a month, from 1 to 30.

The shape to look for is the smile. Chen notes the curve for a social product "shows the characteristic smile shape that indicates there's a group of highly engaged users using the app daily or nearly daily" — a U where the right-hand wall of near-daily users curls back up. That spike is the product's true core, and it's exactly what a blended average destroys. The power user curve "shows if you have a hardcore, engaged segment that's coming back every day" and "shows the variability among your users," letting you cohort and track whether that core is thickening over time. For senior PMs, the practical use is twofold: it's a sharper PMF signal than a single retention number for high-frequency products, and it tells you whether your growth is adding power users or just adding one-time triers to the left wall.

Activation and the aha moment

If retention is the output, activation is the input the canon returns to most. The folklore version is Facebook's "7 friends in 10 days," but Mode's retelling is more honest and more useful: that number, attributed to Chamath Palihapitiya, was a memorable rallying target, not a scientific constant, and "the 'aha moment' is a misnomer... It's a set of actions that separate customers who find value in your product from those who don't." You find it by comparing retained and churned users and isolating the early action that the retained ones almost always take and the churned ones don't — with the discipline that the action must be common enough among the retained and rare enough among the lost to actually discriminate.

The caveat Mode insists on is the one most "aha moment" decks omit: it is a correlation until proven otherwise. "Once you find what correlates most strongly with retention, push users to take that action. If the relationship is causal, more people will retain... If it's not causal, people will take the action but won't retain at higher rates." Forcing the behavior and watching whether retention actually moves is what separates a genuine activation lever from a coincidence — the same A/B caution the cohort section raised, applied to your headline activation metric.

Sean Ellis frames activation as the explicit bridge from acquisition to retention: get the user to "an experience where they actually say, oh my ... this product is amazing," and "eliminate all the unnecessary friction" so they reach it fast. His method is to mine your most successful users for the experience that hooked them (Slack's 2,000 messages sent, Uber's first completed ride). And his must-have survey gives a coarse but durable PMF read alongside the curve: if 40% or more of your users would be "very disappointed" without the product, "you're delivering the 'must-have' experience." Activation, in this camp, is where retention is won or lost — by the time a user has churned in week one, the lever was upstream.

Habit and frequency

The activation camp explains the first return; the habit camp explains the hundredth. Nir Eyal's Hook Model, summarized by Amplitude, is a four-phase loop — Trigger → Action → Variable Reward → Investment — run often enough that it wires itself into the user's routine. The trigger starts external (a notification, an email, an icon) and the goal is to migrate it inward, so that an internal state — boredom, loneliness, uncertainty — becomes the cue. The reward must be variable, because "novelty activates the dopamine system"; Eyal sorts rewards into the Tribe (connection and belonging), the Hunt (tangible resources or information), and the Self (mastery and completion). The Investment phase is the retention hinge: by getting the user to put work in — data, content, social connections, configuration — you both improve the next experience and load the next trigger, so the user "will make another pass through the Hook."

Eyal's deeper argument, in "How to Manufacture Desire," is about what habit buys you commercially: "by cycling continuously through these hooks, users begin to form associations with internal triggers," until "the habit is formed" and users "come to the site without any external prompting." The payoff is that "the first-to-mind solution wins" — habitual products stop depending on paid acquisition because users self-initiate. This is the mechanistic story under the flattening retention curve for high-frequency products: the plateau is the set of users for whom an internal trigger now reliably fires.

The honest framing for senior PMs is that the Hook Model is descriptive and powerful but also contested in application — it works only for genuinely high-frequency use cases (you cannot manufacture a daily habit for software someone needs twice a year), and the same mechanics that drive retention raise the ethics questions Eyal himself later addressed. It is a lens for habit-shaped products, not a universal retention theory.

Accruing benefits and mounting losses

The third causal camp explains retention through switching costs that grow with use. Sarah Tavel's Hierarchy of Engagement is a three-level model — growing engaged users, retaining them, then becoming self-perpetuating — built on a product's core action plus two compounding forces. Accruing benefits means the product gets better the more you use it: a note-taking app with three years of notes, a network that knows your connections, a feed tuned by your history. Mounting losses means leaving costs more over time: the data, history, reputation, or relationships you'd forfeit grow with tenure.

Tavel frames "user engagement as the fuel powering products," and the hierarchy's promise is that as a company climbs it, products "become better, harder to leave, and ultimately create virtuous loops that make the product self-perpetuating." The strategic counsel that comes with this model is a deliberate trade: prioritizing the engagement that creates accruing benefits and mounting losses over raw top-line user growth — slower growth with higher retention compounds into a far more durable business than fast growth that leaks. (Tavel has since published an updated version of the framework; the accruing-benefits / mounting-losses core remains the most-cited part.)

Read together, the three camps are complementary rather than competing: activation gets the user to first value, habit makes return automatic for high-frequency products, and accruing benefits / mounting losses make the product progressively harder to abandon. Most durable products run all three; which one is your dominant lever depends on natural frequency and whether your product accumulates user-specific value.

The retention lifecycle: new, current, resurrected, dormant

Aggregate retention hides not just engagement depth but the flow of users between states, and growth accounting exists to expose it. Amplitude's lifecycle framework segments every active user into New, Current, and Resurrected (active again after a dormant stretch), against Churned/Dormant on the other side of the ledger. The summary statistic is the Quick Ratio = (New + Resurrected) / Churned: above 1 the product is growing its active base, below 1 it's contracting — even if a headline MAU still looks flat, because gross adds can mask gross losses.

The framework's real value is prescriptive: it routes different tactics to different states because "not all active users are created equal." The goal is to move each bucket — new, current, resurrected — toward highly engaged, and the diagnostic questions differ by state: for new users, "how to get users to come back"; for current users, "what your users are (and aren't) doing"; for resurrected users, "why users are coming back." A single retention curve would blur all three populations into one line; the lifecycle view tells you whether your problem is a leaky top (activation), a thinning middle (engagement), or an untapped pool of the lapsed (resurrection).

For senior PMs, growth accounting is the bridge between the retention curve and the roadmap: the curve says whether you retain, the lifecycle says which population to spend the next quarter on, and the Quick Ratio gives leadership a single honest number that resists the vanity of gross adds.

DAU/MAU and the stickiness trap

The DAU/MAU ratio is the most over-trusted retention proxy in the industry, and Charlie Taylor's "Fooled by Stickiness" is the sharpest takedown. The headline problem is that a single ratio blends a heterogeneous population into a number that feels precise and means little: a 34% ratio can coexist with the reality that "most of the users use the product on less than 20% of the days" and "no users use the product on 34% of the days." The 34% is an artifact of averaging, not a description of how anyone actually behaves.

Three failure modes follow. First, it lacks engagement depth — it can't tell you whether your active users are getting real, repeated value or just barely clearing the "active" bar. Second, it moves for the wrong reasons: as MAU grows with one-time triers, the ratio mechanically declines even if your core is perfectly healthy, and conversely a shrinking, concentrating base can make stickiness rise while the business dies. Third, it drives bad bets — Taylor names "prematurely shifting product development onto incremental tweaks" and "prematurely ramping up marketing budget" on the false confidence that new cohorts will replicate the blended ratio. His verdict is quotable: "aggregated DAU/MAU might be good for slideshows, but it's a bad way to measure user behavior and product performance." The fix is the same distribution-first instinct behind the power user curve — ask about core actions, minimum power-user frequency, and per-segment behavior rather than one ratio. DAU/MAU is a fine top-line glance for a genuinely daily product and actively misleading everywhere else, including most B2B and seasonal products.

Tactics to improve retention

When the diagnosis is done, the tactical literature converges on a clear priority order. Lenny Rachitsky's playbook ranks levers roughly: improve the product itself, then onboarding, then stickiness, then pre-churn intervention, then value reminders, then win-back, then better targeting. The empirical anchor is that "almost every big, lasting retention win I've seen came from improving the early user experience" — the first 7 to 30 days — because, as he puts it, "it's very hard to invent sustainable new customer value," so connecting users to existing value faster usually beats building new features. This is the activation camp expressed as a roadmap: most of the retention you can win is won early.

The lifecycle framing assigns tactics to states. New-user activation is onboarding and aha-moment work; engagement and stickiness target current users; and resurrection targets the dormant. Amplitude treats resurrection as one of three retention levers (alongside activation and engagement), with a concrete method: define dormancy by your product's own usage pattern via the lifecycle chart, find "external factors and previous campaigns that brought back some of your dormant users," then run targeted win-back campaigns — email and push to lapsed users with relevant incentives. The framing is that "acquisition won't help your business if you constantly lose new users after onboarding," so plugging the leak precedes refilling the bucket.

The non-obvious sequencing rule both sources share is that resurrection only pays off once the underlying cohort curve has stopped trending to zero. If new cohorts still slide to nothing, a win-back campaign just recycles users through the same broken early experience — they resurrect and re-churn. Resurrection is a lever for a product that retains; for one that doesn't, the spend belongs upstream in activation. This is why "improve onboarding" outranks "win back lapsed users" on every serious list.

Benchmarks and pitfalls

The question every executive asks — "is our retention good?" — is the one the canon most insists you answer carefully. Brian Balfour's warning is that benchmark comparisons are usually invalid because the metrics aren't measured the same way: "CAC is CAC. LTV is LTV. Retention is Retention. Right? Definitely not." What counts as good retention is contingent on the business model — acquisition motion and expansion motion in particular. A consumer app that must acquire enormous numbers to reach scale needs a fundamentally different retention profile than bottoms-up SaaS, where Balfour notes "100% net revenue retention is not good and won't build a venture scale business" because that model lives on expansion.

Several pitfalls recur across the sources. The first is comparing across categories at all — social, SaaS, marketplace, and gaming retention curves are not commensurable, and a "good" number borrowed from another category is meaningless. The second is the definitional mismatch from the metric section: benchmarking N-day retention against a competitor who reports unbounded retention compares two different quantities. The third is survivorship and cohort-mix effects — a retention number can improve simply because your acquisition got more selective, not because the product got stickier. The fourth, which Balfour stresses, is treating retention as something you target directly rather than as the output of inputs: setting a retention OKR without naming the activation or engagement lever behind it invites gaming. The senior-PM posture across all of this is to benchmark a cohort against its own past — is week-4 retention rising cohort over cohort? — far more than against any external number.

Live debates

Several questions in this space are genuinely contested, and a primer should flag them rather than pick a side.

  • Is a flattening curve sufficient evidence of PMF? Schultz treats the asymptote as the operational test, but Balfour's nuance — that the acceptable shape depends entirely on business model — means a flat curve can still describe a business that won't scale. Practitioners agree flattening is necessary; they disagree on how far it's sufficient.
  • Correlation vs causation in the aha moment. The "7 friends in 10 days" genre is widely taught as gospel, but Mode and the cohort literature both stress these are correlations until an A/B test proves forcing the behavior lifts retention. The live disagreement is how much weight to put on a discovered milestone before you've proven it causal — and whether the famous milestones were ever rigorously causal at all.
  • DAU/MAU: useful glance or actively harmful? The ratio remains a board-deck staple, while Taylor and the power-user-curve camp argue it's misleading for most products. There's no settled line on when a single stickiness ratio earns its place versus when it must be replaced by a distribution.
  • Benchmark comparability. A whole sub-industry publishes retention benchmarks, while Balfour argues cross-company comparison is fundamentally flawed. Teams have to decide how much to trust external numbers versus their own cohort-over-cohort trend.
  • Habit-building ethics. The Hook Model's effectiveness is not in dispute; whether and how aggressively to engineer compulsive use is — a debate Eyal himself joined after Hooked. This is rapidly evolving as regulation and norms around attention shift.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Product metrics & driver trees — retention is the canonical output metric; decomposing it into activation, engagement, and resurrection drivers is a driver-tree exercise.
  • Growth loops & the AARRR funnel — retention is the stage every other loop (referral, revenue, acquisition) compounds on; this primer's "core of growth" claim is the loop view's foundation.
  • Activation & onboarding — the highest-leverage retention lever sits upstream in the first-session experience; worth its own deeper treatment.
  • Product/market fit — the flattening retention curve is one of the field's most concrete PMF tests, alongside Sean Ellis's must-have survey.
  • Experimentation & causal inference — the recurring "correlation until A/B-tested" caveat is where retention work meets experiment design.
  • Pricing & monetization — net revenue retention and expansion are the monetization-side mirror of usage retention, and the two can diverge.

References

  1. startuparchive.org ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Alex Schultz (Facebook VP Growth) argues retention is the single most important thing for growth: a retention curve that "asymptotes to a line parallel to the x-axis" proves product/market fit, and "if you do not have a retention curve that flattens out, you do not have product/market fit and none of the growth hacking or virality tactics that people love to talk about will work."

  2. brianbalfour.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Brian Balfour (Reforge) calls retention an output, not a lever — "Retention Is An Output" driven by inputs like activation — and warns benchmark comparisons are inherently flawed because "CAC is CAC. LTV is LTV. Retention is Retention. Right? Definitely not": what counts as good retention depends on acquisition and expansion motion, so cross-category comparison misleads.

  3. andrewchen.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Andrew Chen's power user curve (the L30 activity histogram) plots users by number of active days in a month; the characteristic "smile shape" — a cluster of near-daily power users on the right — "indicates there's a group of highly engaged users using the app daily or nearly daily," surfacing the engagement distribution that a single DAU/MAU average hides.

  4. lennysnewsletter.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Olga Berezovsky (via Lenny) frames cohort retention as a color-coded triangle table showing "how many users registered each week and how many of them were retained in each subsequent week"; the meaningful patterns emerge across cohorts, and retention is an output metric reflecting the cumulative product experience, with the flattening point revealing the natural retention equilibrium.

  5. amplitude.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Amplitude shows behavioral cohorts isolating retention drivers: in a music app "almost 40% of all users churn within one day" but "only about 15% of users who favorite three or more songs drop off after day one," and inverted/combined cohorts (favoriting + community) lift retention above 75% vs under 25% without either — then A/B test to confirm causation rather than assume it.

  6. medium.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Amplitude lays out three retention definitions — N-day (returned on a specific day, fits daily-habit products like mobile games), unbounded/rolling (returned on or any time after a day, fits sporadic products like grocery delivery), and bracketed (custom intervals) — and the matching principle: pick the type that matches your product's natural usage frequency.

  7. amplitude.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Nir Eyal's Hook Model (via Amplitude) cycles Trigger (external → internal) → Action → Variable Reward → Investment; rewards split into Tribe ("connection or belonging"), Hunt (tangible resources), and Self (mastery), and "novelty activates the dopamine system," while the Investment phase loads the next trigger — repeated cycles build the habit that drives retention.

  8. nirandfar.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Nir Eyal argues habit-forming products move users from external triggers ("an email, a link on a web site, or the app icon on a phone") to internal ones: "by cycling continuously through these hooks, users begin to form associations with internal triggers" until "the habit is formed" and "the first-to-mind solution wins" — users self-initiate without marketing.

  9. sarahtavel.medium.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Sarah Tavel's Hierarchy of Engagement has three levels — growing engaged users, retaining them, and becoming self-perpetuating — built on completing the core action plus accruing benefits and mounting losses; she frames "user engagement as the fuel powering products," where climbing the hierarchy makes products "better, harder to leave, and ultimately create virtuous loops that make the product self-perpetuating."

  10. amplitude.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Amplitude's growth-accounting lifecycle segments users into New, Current, Resurrected, and Churned/Dormant states; the Quick Ratio = (New + Resurrected) / Churned reads >1 as growing and <1 as contracting, and the prescription is segment-specific — for new users ask "how to get users to come back," for resurrected users investigate "why users are coming back" — because "not all active users are created equal."

  11. amplitude.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Amplitude treats resurrection as one of three retention levers (alongside new-user activation and engagement/stickiness): define dormancy by your product's usage pattern via the lifecycle chart, find "external factors and previous campaigns that brought back some of your dormant users," then run targeted win-back campaigns — because "acquisition won't help your business if you constantly lose new users after onboarding."

  12. medium.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Charlie Taylor shows DAU/MAU "stickiness" misleads: a 34% ratio can hide that "most of the users use the product on less than 20% of the days" and "no users use the product on 34% of the days," so "aggregated DAU/MAU might be good for slideshows, but it's a bad way to measure user behavior" — it blends heterogeneous users and triggers premature product and marketing bets.

  13. userpilot.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Userpilot reads the retention curve in three parts: a steep early drop (users who never hit the aha moment, e.g. "falling from 26.5% on Day 1 to 12% by Day 7"), a flattening plateau ("the flat section shows your product stickiness" = the terminal retention rate and product-market fit), versus a curve that keeps sliding to zero — "your product fails to solve a recurring problem or form a habit."

  14. mode.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Mode reframes the aha moment: Facebook's "7 friends in 10 days" (Chamath Palihapitiya) was a memorable rallying target, and "the 'aha moment' is a misnomer... It's a set of actions that separate customers who find value in your product from those who don't" — found by comparing retained vs churned users, with the explicit caveat that the relationship may be correlation, not causation, until forcing the action proves it lifts retention.

  15. userpilot.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Sean Ellis positions activation as the bridge from acquisition to retention — get users to "an experience where they actually say, oh my ... this product is amazing" — found by mining your most engaged users (Slack's 2,000 messages, Facebook's 7 friends), and validates product/market fit with his must-have survey: if 40%+ would be "very disappointed" without the product, "you're delivering the 'must-have' experience."

  16. lennysnewsletter.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Lenny Rachitsky's retention playbook ranks tactics by impact — improve the product, then onboarding, stickiness, pre-churn intervention, value reminders, win-back, and better targeting — built on the finding that "almost every big, lasting retention win I've seen came from improving the early user experience" (the first 7–30 days), and that resurrection only works if the cohort curve isn't already trending to zero.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_retention.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
